# Agent System — Quick Reference

## What this system is

Eight agent files, three shell scripts, and a set of shared documents
that together prevent cognitive debt, maintain architectural integrity,
and keep codebases understandable to any fresh engineer or AI agent.

Designed for solo non-coders using agentic AI, but works equally for
small teams. Generic enough to drop into any repo. Smart enough to
compile itself to a repo-specific operating model via `@init`.

**Core principle: code must remain human-auditable at all times.**
AI can write code faster than humans can understand it. This system
pushes back against that directly. Every agent in this system treats
human readability as a first-class requirement, not a nice-to-have.

Default thresholds (set in REPO.context.md, adjustable per repo):
- 50 lines per function or module
- 500 lines per file or component
- Exceptions allowed — but must be documented in the decision record
- Simplicity over cleverness: a non-specialist should be able to read
  any function and explain what it does

---

## Three-layer architecture

This system is designed as three layers, not a flat set of files.

### Layer 1 — Framework core (these files, stable across all repos)

The operating system. Generic, reusable, not modified per repo.

### Layer 2 — Repo profile and extension artifacts (generated by @init)

Created by `@init` inside the target repo. The generic agents read these
if they exist, picking up repo-specific behaviour without framework changes:
- `docs/agent-system/repo-profile.md` — stack, validators, special workflows
- `docs/agent-system/validation-registry.md` — all validator commands
- `docs/agent-system/workflow-registry.md` — special workflow definitions
- `docs/agent-system/hook-registry.md` — Copilot and git hooks in use
- `docs/agent-system/customization-inventory.md` — existing customization surface

### Layer 3 — Optional repo-specific wrappers (generated by @init on request)

When a repo has complex special workflows or existing custom agents,
`@init` can generate thin wrappers such as:
- `.github/agents/<repo>-code.agent.md`
- `.github/agents/<repo>-phase-gate.agent.md`

Wrappers keep the generic step flow intact but prepend the repo profile
read order and add repo-specific completion gates. They do not fork
the framework — they extend it.

---

## The eight agents

| Agent | When to run | Which model |
|---|---|---|
| `init.agent` | Once per project; `@init ingest` on mature repos | Any |
| `code.agent` | Every bug fix, feature, or dev phase | Primary model |
| `phase.gate.agent` | After every code.agent session | **Different model** |
| `testing.error.report.agent` | After phase.gate passes, or on test failures | **Different model** |
| `cold.review.agent` | Monthly or pre-deployment | **Different model, cold context** |
| `agent-creation.agent` | When creating or auditing AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md | Any |
| `skill-creation.agent` | When capturing a workflow into a reusable skill | Any |
| `cleanup.agent` | Systematic sweeps: auditing, dead code, line-count cleanup | Varies — see agent |

---

## The shared documents

### Always created by @init (every repo)

| File | Created by | Read by | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `REPO.context.md` | init.agent | all agents | Project context, critical paths, constraints |
| `CHANGES.md` | init.agent | all agents | Decision records, audit trail, shared memory |
| `docs/guardrails/baseline.md` | init.agent | code.agent, phase.gate | Architectural rules and do-not-break baseline |
| `docs/tests/plan.md` | init.agent | code.agent, testing.error.report | Test coverage status and history |
| `docs/invariants.md` | init.agent | code.agent, phase.gate, cold.review | System truths that must never be violated |
| `docs/incidents.md` | init.agent | code.agent, phase.gate, cold.review | Past failure patterns to avoid repeating |

### Generated by @init when content exists (repo extension layer)

| File | Created when | Read by | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `docs/agent-system/repo-profile.md` | every repo | code, phase.gate, testing | Stack, validators, special workflows |
| `docs/agent-system/validation-registry.md` | validators found | code, phase.gate, testing | Exact validator commands per phase |
| `docs/agent-system/workflow-registry.md` | special workflows found | code, phase.gate, testing | Special workflow definitions |
| `docs/agent-system/hook-registry.md` | hooks found | init (install-hooks) | Copilot and git hooks in use |
| `docs/agent-system/customization-inventory.md` | ingest mode, or when existing customization is found | cold.review | Existing customization surface |

---

## The shell scripts

These are framework utilities deployed into the target repo's `scripts/` directory.
They are called by the agents but are not agents themselves.

| Script | Purpose | When called |
|---|---|---|
| `preflight.sh` | Pre-change safety check — snapshot if STRUCTURAL | code.agent Step 0 |
| `snapshot.sh` | Creates timestamped snapshot of working state | code.agent on STRUCTURAL tasks |
| `probe-stack.sh` | Detects stack, test runner, linter, dependencies | init.agent scan support |

---

## Framework templates

These stay in the framework package and are used by `@init` to generate
repo-specific automation assets without hardcoding one repo's setup into
another repo.

| Template | Generates | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `templates/hooks/pre-commit.sh.template` | `scripts/hooks/pre-commit.sh` | Generic pre-commit validator hook |
| `templates/hooks/session-start.sh.template` | `scripts/hooks/session-start.sh` | Session-start context hook |
| `templates/hooks/post-edit.sh.template` | `scripts/hooks/post-edit.sh` | Post-edit advisory hook |
| `templates/install-git-hooks.sh.template` | `scripts/install-git-hooks.sh` | Safe git-hook installer |
| `templates/copilot-hooks.json.template` | `.github/hooks/<repo>.json` | Copilot hook manifest |
| `templates/sync-agent-system.py.template` | `scripts/sync_agent_system.py` | Doc-to-config sync helper |

---

## Standard workflow

### New project
```
@init
→ creates REPO.context.md, CHANGES.md, docs/guardrails/, docs/tests/
→ creates docs/invariants.md and docs/incidents.md skeletons
→ creates docs/agent-system/repo-profile.md and any applicable registries
→ may generate repo automation assets from framework templates
→ follow its output instructions
```

### Mature project (first time adding this framework)
```
@init ingest
→ scans existing agents, hooks, prompts, validators
→ builds customization-inventory.md
→ creates/updates extension files preserving existing knowledge
→ recommends keep / wrap / replace for each existing artifact
```

### Dev phase
```
@code phase: 1 [attach dev plan]
→ reads repo-profile.md and registries if present
→ completes all steps, writes decision record to CHANGES.md

@phase.gate          ← different model
→ reads registries to verify against actual repo validators
→ verifies change, appends verdict to CHANGES.md

@testing.error.report ← different model
→ reads validation and workflow registries
→ audits tests, appends report to CHANGES.md
```

### Bug fix
```
@code fix: <describe bug>
→ completes all steps, writes decision record

@phase.gate          ← different model
→ verifies fix

@testing.error.report ← different model (if tests fail or complex fix)
```

### Monthly / pre-deploy
```
@cold.review         ← different model, NO prior conversation context
→ reads customization-inventory.md if present
→ cognitive debt assessment, appends to CHANGES.md
```

---

## Model diversity — why it matters

Same model reviewing its own output will normalise its own mistakes.
The value of phase.gate and cold.review depends entirely on using
a different model. A rough guide:

- code.agent on Claude → phase.gate on GPT or Gemini
- code.agent on local model → phase.gate on cloud model (or vice versa)
- cold.review → whichever model you use least, with zero prior context

---

## File placement

### Copilot
```
.github/agents/
  init.agent.md
  code.agent.md
  phase.gate.agent.md
  testing.error.report.agent.md
  cold.review.agent.md
  agent-creation.agent.md
  skill-creation.agent.md
  cleanup.agent.md

scripts/
  preflight.sh
  snapshot.sh
  probe-stack.sh

templates/
  hooks/pre-commit.sh.template
  hooks/session-start.sh.template
  hooks/post-edit.sh.template
  install-git-hooks.sh.template
  copilot-hooks.json.template
  sync-agent-system.py.template
```

### OpenCode
```
.opencode/agents/
  init.md
  code.md
  phase.gate.md
  testing.error.report.md
  cold.review.md
  agent-creation.md
  skill-creation.md
  cleanup.md
```

---

## CHANGES.md entry anatomy

A complete entry after a full workflow looks like this:

```
---
DATE:        2026-03-07
MODE:        BUG FIX
SCOPE:       CONTAINED
WHAT:        Fixed token refresh failure on provider 504 responses.
WHY:         Added retry with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts.
             Considered circuit breaker — deferred to later phase.
EDGE:        If all 3 retries fail, user sees login error. No silent fail.
TESTS:       Added: test_token_refresh_504_retry. Affected: none.
MECH:        Lint: pass. Types: pass. Coverage: 84%. Security: pass.
BREAKS:      None identified — verify do-not-break list manually.
SIZE:        Largest function: 22 lines. Largest file: 180 lines.
INTERFACES:  None.
ROLLBACK:    auth/token.py line 47–83, function refresh_token().
MODEL:       claude-sonnet-4-6
---
PHASE GATE:  PASS — 2026-03-07 — gpt-4o
FINDINGS:    None
TEST AUDIT:  ADEQUATE — 2026-03-07 — gemini-2.0-flash
FINDINGS:    Happy path bias noted in existing tests — not blocking
```

---

## When to update REPO.context.md

- After any STRUCTURAL change
- When critical paths change
- When adding a new major dependency
- When the dev plan evolves significantly
- Run `@init update` — it extends rather than overwrites

---

## Git workflow

This framework enforces a simple, auditable git discipline so that AI-generated
work produces a history a human can follow without archaeology.

### Branch-per-task rule

`code.agent` always checks the current branch in Step 0b before writing any
code. If on `main` or `master`, it creates a feature branch first.

| Task type | Branch name pattern |
|---|---|
| Bug fix | `fix/<short-slug>` |
| Feature / dev phase | `feat/<slug>` or `feat/phase-<n>-<slug>` |
| Infrastructure / chore | `chore/<slug>` |
| Refactor | `refactor/<slug>` |

Slug convention: 2–4 words, hyphen-separated, lowercase.

### One commit per task

After each task's decision record is written (Step 9), `code.agent` creates
one commit for that task using conventional commit format:

```
<type>(<scope>): <imperative description>

- bullet: what changed
- bullet: why

Verified: lint pass · types pass · tests 42/42
```

This means each commit in the history corresponds to exactly one decision
record in CHANGES.md — a one-to-one audit trail.

### Push rule

- Never push to `main` / `master` directly.
- Push the feature branch; create a PR or merge manually when ready.
- Offer to push after each task commit, but never push without confirmation.

### Readable history convention

A well-shaped history for a feature looks like:
```
feat/phase-1-ollama-client
  feat(client): implement Ollama completion endpoint
  test(client): add stability-metric unit tests
  fix(client): handle 504 timeout in retry loop
→ merge into main as: feat: phase 1 complete — Ollama client
```

---

## What this system does not solve

- Fundamentally misaligned model behaviour
- Incorrect architectural decisions that pass all checks (Dijkstra problem)
- Security vulnerabilities in AI-generated code (use a dedicated scanner)
- Formal correctness guarantees

The cold.review agent catches the third class partially.
The oversight note in code.agent Step 4 catches the second partially.
Neither is a substitute for a domain expert reviewing STRUCTURAL changes.
